stocks.note: Bausch & Lomb (eye-care). Taken private by Warburg Pincus, deal closed Oct 26, 2007 at $65.00/share cash. Traded roughly $35-80 across the 2000s; dipped in 2002-03 on accounting issues, recovered, then a 2006 ReNu MoistureLoc recall pressured the stock before the buyout. Acquirer was a private equity firm (no public ticker). Anchors are clean split-adjusted prices for the original NYSE:BOL.

Cooper Industries (electrical products, Irish-domiciled). Acquired by Eaton Corp; deal closed Nov 30, 2012. Each Cooper share received $39.15 cash plus 0.77479 Eaton shares, total deal value ~$72/share. Cooper underwent a ~2-for-1 split in 2006 (the dip from ~$60 in Jan 2005 to ~$36.50 in Jan 2006 reflects that adjusted basis). All anchors on one consistent split-adjusted basis; traded roughly $26-84 across the period.

Countrywide Financial (mortgage lender). Acquired by Bank of America in all-stock deal, completed July 1, 2008; 0.1822 BAC share per CFC share (~$4-7 implied at close). Stock peaked ~$45 in early 2007 then collapsed through the subprime crisis: ~$18-19 by Aug 2007, $8.64 on Nov 26 2007, ~$7.75 at the Jan 11 2008 deal announcement, ~$4-5 by the July 2008 close. Clean split-adjusted basis for NYSE:CFC.

Fisher Scientific International (lab supplies/instruments). Merged with Thermo Electron to form Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), closed Nov 9, 2006. All-stock merger of equals; each Fisher share converted to ~2.0 shares of the combined Thermo Fisher. Fisher traded roughly $28-80 over 2001-2006, climbing strongly from 2004 onward. Clean split-adjusted basis for NYSE:FSH.

Golden West Financial (savings & loan; World Savings parent). Acquired by Wachovia (WB), closed Oct 1, 2006. Cash-and-stock deal: each GDW share received ~1.051 Wachovia shares plus ~$18.65 cash, total ~$70-80/share at announcement. Traded roughly $27-77 across 2001-2006 in a steady uptrend. Clean split-adjusted basis for NYSE:GDW. (Wachovia itself was later absorbed by Wells Fargo in 2008.)

Goodrich Corporation (aerospace systems/components). Acquired by United Technologies (UTX) for $127.50/share cash; deal closed July 26, 2012. The $127.50 was a ~47% premium to the ~$87 close before Sept 2011 rumors. Stock troughed near $15 in 2002-03 (post-9/11 aerospace slump), recovered to ~$70 by 2008, dipped in 2009, then climbed steadily; once the deal was announced it traded just under $127.50. Clean split-adjusted basis for NYSE:GR. UTX later became Raytheon Technologies (RTX).

Knight-Ridder (newspaper publisher). Acquired by The McClatchy Company (MNI), closed June 27, 2006. Each KRI share received $40.00 cash plus 0.5118 of an MNI Class A share, ~$64-67/share total. Stock peaked ~$78 in 2004, drifted lower on newspaper-industry pressure, closed at $63.92 the day the deal was announced (Mar 13 2006). Clean split-adjusted basis for NYSE:KRI.

Palm, Inc. (smartphones/PDAs; the post-2003 spinoff that took the Palm name after palmOne renamed in 2005). Acquired by Hewlett-Packard (HPQ) for $5.70/share cash; deal closed July 1, 2010. Anchors use the post-2005 1-for-20 reverse-split basis (~$30s in late 2005, falling into the teens by 2007, ~$3-5 in 2008-09, a WebOS-hype spike to ~$18 in 2009, then back below $5 before the HP bid). Pre-2005 dot-com-era highs are deliberately excluded as they are a different (pre-reverse-split) basis. Medium confidence on exact daily marks given thin sourcing, but magnitudes are correct.

The Pepsi Bottling Group (anchor bottler). Acquired by PepsiCo (PEP), closed Feb 26, 2010. PBG holders elected cash or stock subject to proration around a ~$36.50/share value; standard mix approximated $12.50 cash plus 0.5 PEP share per PBG share. Traded roughly $20-45 over 2001-2010, dipping in the 2008-09 crisis then recovering toward the deal value. Clean split-adjusted basis for NYSE:PBG.
